interest: It bundles the interests of non-state (church/foundation) maintainers defending their autonomy and enrollment discretion with those of general-school pupils, teachers, and families who bear the overcrowding and redeployment costs if compensatory capacity is not funded on time.
value: Equity (reducing early sorting) is in tension with maintainer autonomy/subsidiarity and with the child-wellbeing and delivery-feasibility values that guaranteed capacity is meant to protect.
fear: That the reform will merely relocate stratification into less-regulated non-state tracks while dumping unfunded overcrowding and forced teacher redeployment onto general schools already in a documented shortage.
affected: general-school pupils (especially lower-SES pupils reliant on residual selective places)
the governing majority legislating the schedule
assumption: That the equity gain depends on legal, enforceable regulatory levers over non-state maintainers existing (or being validatable in advance) and on compensatory capacity funding being ring-fenced and disbursed ahead of each year's place reduction.
empirical_uncertainty: Contested-to-unknown: the scenario's own record concedes the constraining regulatory levers do not currently exist and are legally untested against maintainer agreements, and treats non-state absorption of displaced demand as a structural likelihood rather than a settled outcome.
